document_metadata.confidence_calibration: Uncalibrated - will adjust after 10+ pilot analyses

findings.title: Conflicting Compensation for Executed Contracts
findings.explanation:
Section 3, Milestones & Deliverables table: For the 'Contracts executed — early adopter cohort' milestone, the Compensation Trigger is '3% of up-front revenue captured for early adopter cohort'.

vs

EXHIBIT A — COMPENSATION SCHEDULE table: For the 'Signed contracts w/ monetary payment' milestone, the Bonus Amount is '$__________'.

Problem: There is a direct and material contradiction regarding the bonus for securing signed contracts. Section 3 defines the compensation as a variable percentage based on revenue, while Exhibit A defines it as a fixed—but unspecified—cash amount. This makes a key compensation term completely unenforceable and guarantees a dispute. The Contractor has no clear understanding of their potential earnings for achieving a primary objective.

findings.title: Ambiguous Ownership of Developed Intellectual Property
findings.explanation:
Section 9.1 Work Product: All deliverables, materials, and work product created by the Contractor specifically for [Company Name] in the performance of services under this SOW shall be considered work made for hire and shall be the sole property of [Company Name].

vs

Section 9.2 LLM Framework — Contractor IP: Any enhancements, adaptations, or derivatives of the Contractor IP developed during this engagement shall be addressed in the Second SOW or a separate IP agreement, should the parties elect to proceed with formal integration.

Problem: The agreement defers the critical decision of who owns 'enhancements, adaptations, or derivatives' of the Contractor's IP. The core of the work involves creating a 'roadmap', 'testing strategy', and 'initial test results' (Sec 2.1) using Company resources. These items could be considered 'work product' under Section 9.1 (owned by the Company) or 'enhancements' under Section 9.2 (ownership deferred). If the parties do not execute a second SOW, there is no agreed-upon mechanism to assign ownership of the valuable IP co-developed during the trial, creating a high probability of a legal dispute.

findings.title: Pervasive Use of Unresolved Placeholders
findings.explanation:
Multiple Sections: The document is riddled with unresolved placeholders, including '[Company Name]', '[DATE]', '[START DATE]', '[END DATE]', '[Last Name]', and a blank compensation amount ('$__________') in Exhibit A.

vs

ENGAGEMENT OVERVIEW: The document identifies the client as 'UtopIQ, a Schmidt-Morgan Corporation', but then repeatedly uses '[Company Name]' throughout the agreement, including in the signature blocks.

Problem: The presence of numerous material placeholders indicates the document is an unfinalized draft. As it stands, the agreement is likely legally unenforceable. A contract with a party identified as '[Company Name]' or with a payment term of '$__________' cannot be enforced in court. It fails to clearly identify the parties and their fundamental obligations.

findings.title: Contradictory and Incomplete Agreement Dates
findings.explanation:
ENGAGEMENT OVERVIEW: Effective Date 3/30/26 ... Initial Term Three (3) months — Trial Period ending 6/30/26

vs

Section 5.1 Trial Period: The initial term of this engagement ('Trial Period') commences on [START DATE] and runs for three (3) months, through [END DATE].

Problem: The document provides a specific start date (3/30/26) in the overview but uses an unresolved placeholder '[START DATE]' in the governing Term section (Section 5.1). This creates a legal contradiction as to when the agreement actually begins, making all subsequent milestone deadlines (e.g., April 17, May 27) ambiguous and potentially unenforceable.

findings.title: Ambiguous Definition of 'Committed Capital' for Bonus Trigger
findings.explanation:
Section 3, Milestones & Deliverables table: Milestone Target: 'Early adopter onboarding supported; $1M raised (non-dilutive and/or dilutive)'

vs

Section 3, Milestones & Deliverables table: Success Metric: 'Onboarding underway; at least $1M in committed capital raised by June 8'

Problem: The term 'committed capital' is a critical, undefined term for a milestone with a $30,000 bonus (per Exhibit A). This ambiguity creates a financial risk. Does 'committed' mean a signed term sheet for investment, or does it require the cash to be in the company's bank account? A dispute is likely if the company secures a term sheet by the deadline but the funds have not yet been transferred, as the parties will interpret the term to their own advantage.

findings.title: Subjective and Potentially Unachievable Milestone
findings.explanation:
Section 2.1 Goal: '...produce a targeted Q3 roadmap and initial test results that demonstrate whether and how the LLM assessment framework can be formally incorporated...' 

vs

Section 3, Milestones & Deliverables table: Success Metric for the LLM discovery milestone is an 'LOI drafted by Company & expressing interest and intent to support development...'

Problem: The scope of work (Section 2.1) correctly anticipates that the 2-month discovery may prove the framework is not a fit. However, the associated success metric (Section 3) is entirely dependent on the Company's subjective 'interest and intent' to proceed. This creates a scenario where the Contractor can perform all required work perfectly but fail the milestone because the Company makes a unilateral business decision not to proceed. Tying a success metric to a party's subjective feelings rather than objective completion of tasks is a significant risk for the performing party.
